concepts.title: Some properties based on Inequality
concepts.conceptBody:
Some properties based on Inequality

1. If $f\left( x \right)\le 0$ on an interval $\left[ a,b \right]$, then

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
{} & \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx\le 0 \\
{} & \text{ or }\text{ if }f\left( x \right)\ge 0,\text{ then }\mathop{ \int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx\ge 0 \\
\end{array}$

The reason is very obvious because if $f\left( x \right)\ge 0$ the area will be above $x$-axis i.e. positive & for $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)<0$ it will be negative.

2. Property-14 : 

The proof is similar to the reasoning given in the above case.

3. Property-15 : If $\text{m}$ and $\text{M}$ are the smallest & largest values of function $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ defined on an interval $\left[ \text{a},\text{b} \right]$ then

Proof. It is given that

$\text{m}\le \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\le \text{M}$

integrating both sides

$\begin{matrix}
{} & \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}mdx\le \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx\le \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}Mdx \\
\Rightarrow & m\left( b-a \right)\le \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx\le M\left( b-a \right) \\
\end{matrix}$

Property-16 : If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is defined over [a,b] then

\(\left|\int_a^b f(x) g(x) d x\right| \leq\left(\int_a^b f^2(x) d x\right)\left(\int_a^b g^2(x) d x\right)^{1 / 2}\)

 

This is very straight forward result.

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ So }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx \right|=\mid \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ area }\!\!~\!\!\text{ OBC }\!\!~\!\!\text{ - }\!\!~\!\!\text{ area }\!\!~\!\!\text{ OAD }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mid $ …(i)

Whereas

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{\text{a}}^{\text{b}}\left| \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) \right|\text{dx}=$ area OBC + area OAD …(ii)

compare (i) and (ii) to get to the result.

Property-17 : If ${{\text{f}}^{2}}\left( \text{x} \right)\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{g}}^{2}}\left( \text{x} \right)$ are integrable over [a,b] then

\(\left|\int_a^b f(x) g(x) d x\right| \leq\left(\int_a^b f^2(x) d x\right)\left(\int_a^b g^2(x) d x\right)^{1 / 2}\)

Illustration 

(a) Show that $\left| \int _{10}^{19}\frac{\text{sin}x}{1+{{x}^{8}}}dx \right|<\frac{1}{{{10}^{7}}}$ (b) Prove that $\frac{\pi }{6}\le \int _{0}^{1}\frac{\text{dx}}{\sqrt{4-{{\text{x}}^{2}}-\text{x}}}\le \frac{\pi }{4\sqrt{2}}$

Solution : 

  1. $\left| \int _{10}^{19}\frac{\text{sin}x}{1+{{x}^{8}}}dx \right|\le \int _{10}^{19}\left| \frac{\text{sin}x}{1+{{x}^{8}}} \right|dx$ $\left[ \because \left| \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx \right|\le \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}\left| f\left( x \right) \right|dx \right]$

$=\mathop{\int }_{10}^{19}\frac{\left| \text{sin}x \right|}{1+{{x}^{8}}}dx$

$\le \mathop{\int }_{10}^{19}\frac{1}{1+{{x}^{8}}}dx\le \mathop{\int }_{10}^{19}\frac{1}{{{x}^{8}}}dx$ $[|\because| \sin x \mid \leq 1]$

$=\left[ -\frac{1}{7{{x}^{7}}} \right]_{10}^{19}=\frac{1}{7.{{(10)}^{7}}}-\frac{1}{7.{{(19)}^{7}}}$ $<\frac{1}{7.{{(10)}^{7}}}<\frac{1}{{{10}^{7}}}$

  1. $0\le x\le 1$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore & 4-{{x}^{2}}\ge 4-{{x}^{2}}-{{x}^{3}}\ge 4-{{x}^{2}}-{{x}^{2}} \\ \Rightarrow & 4-{{x}^{2}}\ge 4-{{x}^{2}}-{{x}^{3}}\ge 4-2{{x}^{2}}>1 \\ \Rightarrow & 4-{{x}^{2}}\ge 4-{{x}^{2}}-{{x}^{3}}\ge \sqrt{4-2{{x}^{2}}} \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{4-{{x}^{2}}}\ge \sqrt{4-{{x}^{2}}-{{x}^{3}}}>\sqrt{4-2{{x}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$
$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{4-{{x}^{2}}}}\le \frac{1}{\sqrt{4-{{x}^{2}}-{{x}^{3}}}}\le \frac{1}{\sqrt{4-2{{x}^{2}}}} \\ \Rightarrow \mathop{ \int }_{0}^{1}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{4-{{x}^{2}}}}\le \mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{4-{{x}^{2}}-{{x}^{3}}}}\le \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{2-{{x}^{2}}}} \\ \Rightarrow  \left[ \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{x}{2} \right]_{0}^{1}\le \text{I}\le \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[ \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{\text{x}}{\sqrt{2}} \right]_{0}^{1} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{6}\le \text{I}\le \frac{\pi }{4\sqrt{2}} \\ \end{array}$

Illustration

(a) Prove that $4\le \int _{1}^{3}\sqrt{3+{{x}^{3}}}dx\le 2\sqrt{30}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ (b) Prove that $\int _{0}^{1}\sqrt{\left( 1+x \right)\left( 1+{{\pi }^{3}} \right)}dx\le \sqrt{\frac{15}{8}}$. 

Solution : Let $y=\sqrt{3+{{x}^{3}}}$, then $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{-3{{x}^{2}}}{2\sqrt{3+{{x}^{3}}}}>0$

$\therefore \text{ y}$ is an increasing function

$\therefore 1\le \text{x}\le 3$

$\Rightarrow \sqrt{3+{{1}^{3}}}\le \sqrt{3+{{x}^{3}}}\le \sqrt{3+{{3}^{3}}}$

$\Rightarrow 2\le \sqrt{3+{{x}^{3}}}\le \sqrt{30}$

$\Rightarrow \int _{1}^{3}2\text{dx}\le \int _{1}^{3}\sqrt{3+{{\text{x}}^{3}}}\text{dx}\le \sqrt{30}\int _{1}^{3}\text{dx}$

$\Rightarrow 4\le \int _{1}^{3}\sqrt{3+{{x}^{3}}}\text{dx}\le 2\sqrt{30}$

  1. Let $f\left( x \right)=\sqrt{1+x}$ and $g\left( x \right)=\sqrt{1+{{x}^{3}}}$

If ${{\text{f}}^{2}}\left( \text{x} \right)$ and ${{\text{g}}^{2}}\left( \text{x} \right)$ and $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ are integrable functions on [a,b], then

\(\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}  \left| \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)g\left( x \right)dx \right|\le \sqrt{\left( \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}{{f}^{2}}\left( x \right)dx \right)\left( \mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}{{g}^{2}}\left( x \right)dx \right) } \\ \therefore  \mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\sqrt{\left( 1+x \right)\left( 1+{{x}^{2}} \right)}dx\le \sqrt{\left( \mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left( 1+x \right)dx\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left( 1+{{x}^{3}} \right)dx \right)} \\ \end{array}\)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}  =\sqrt{\left[ x+\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2} \right]_{0}^{1}\left[ x+\frac{{{x}^{4}}}{4} \right]_{0}^{1}} \\  =\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}\cdot \frac{5}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{15}{8}} \\ \end{array}$

Thus, $\int _{0}^{1}\sqrt{\left( 1+\text{x} \right)\left( 1+{{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)}\text{dx}\le \sqrt{\frac{15}{8}}$.

examples.title: Question: Let $f(x)$ be a continuous function on the interval $[a, b]$, and let $c$ be a constant such that $a \lt c \lt b$. Which of the following statements is true regarding the definite integrals $\int_a^b f(x) d x$ and $\int_a^c f(x) d x$ ?
Which of the following statements regarding definite integration is true for $f(x)$ defined on the interval $[a, b]$ ?
